comments.text: GPT-5.2 Thinking has extended Woett's results a little bit in this note (edit: now this is the updated version). Specifically, the infinite-area result is sharpened and certified regions are expanded. However, no new main idea has been obtained. (This is probably my last work with GPT-5.2 Thinking.)

A few comments: 1) The theorem that the sequence is complete for all $1 < \alpha \le 1 + \frac{1}{\lceil t \rceil + 2 \lceil \sqrt{t} \rceil}$ seems to be improved such that the denominator $\lceil t \rceil + 2 \lceil \sqrt{t} \rceil$ decreases to some $D$ with $D = \lceil t \rceil + 2 \lceil \sqrt{t} \rceil - f(t)$, with $0 \le f(t) < C$ for some absolute constant $C$. I suppose this counts as an improvement, but it seems rather marginal. Especially since the corresponding $\alpha$ regions for all the small $t$ values (say $t \le 100$) can be quickly checked by other means (e.g. a computer) anyway. 2) On the topic of computer verification, the certified regions for $\alpha \ge 1.4$ are not all new. Table $2$ states for example that the sequence is complete for all pairs $(\alpha, t)$ with $\alpha \in [1.55, 1.60]$ and $t \in [1, 1.15]$. These values are however already covered by Proposition $5$ of my paper. 3) More importantly, the expansion of the certified regions is very minimal. For example, again referring to Table $2$, the sequence is verified to be complete for all pairs $(\alpha, t)$ with $\alpha \in [1.10, 1.15]$ and $t \in [1, 16]$. Using the Python code I put on GitHub, it only takes my laptop $27$ seconds to prove the completeness of the sequence for this entire region. Expanding the $t$-values further to e.g. $[1, 20]$ takes an additional $39$ seconds. This is to say, anyone with a computer can easily do a whole lot more. I'm interested to see what someone with a supercomputer or with the patience to leave their computer running for a few days would get. 4) I apologize for my dismissive tone. I'm at least happy to hear that my calculations seem to have been independently verified, so thanks!

Thanks for the comments! 1) Yes, this is stated in Remark 1 that the gain is "rounding-level". However, there is real improvement for small $t$. 2) Thanks for catching this one; I miss it while reviewing ($T=1.15$ is within known region). 3) The computations are capped (arbitrarily) at 30 seconds at my request because GPT's sandbox has a runtime cap of 60 seconds, so that it doesn't waste time repeatedly timing out commands. The computations were not meant to be with the maximum available compute.
I have clarified that $\alpha \in [1.55,1.60]$ row of Table 2 is already known. Here is the updated note.

I have just uploaded my paper on this problem to the arXiv. Combined with (and relying on) the results from Graham's 1964 paper, the case $\alpha \ge \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}$ is completely settled. Moreover, even though my paper does not fully resolve the problem for the remaining interval $1 < \alpha < \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}$, I can at least give the full range of values of $t$ for which the resulting sequence is entirely complete, i.e. covers all positive integers. In the spirit of Graham, here you can find a plot for part of the 2D plane which shows where the sequence is and isn't (entirely) complete. To describe it in words, this is the current state of affairs: If $\alpha > 2$, the sequence is not complete for any $t > 0$. If $\alpha = 2$, the sequence is complete if, and only if, $t = \frac{1}{2^k}$ for some $k \ge 1$. If $5^{1/3} < \alpha < 2$ and $t \ge 1$, the sequence is not complete. If $5^{1/3} < \alpha < 2$ and $t < 1$, it's complicated (but essentially known). See Graham's paper or the above image for more details. If $\frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \le \alpha \le 5^{1/3}$, the sequence is complete if, and only if, $t < \min\left(\frac{3}{\alpha^2}, \frac{5}{\alpha^3} \right)$. If $1 < \alpha < \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}$, the sequence is entirely complete if, and only if $t < \min\left(\frac{2}{\alpha}, \frac{3}{\alpha^2} \right)$. If $1 < \alpha < \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}$ and $t \ge \min\left(\frac{2}{\alpha}, \frac{3}{\alpha^2} \right)$, the sequence might be complete without being entirely complete, but only partial results are known (see below). If $\alpha = 1$, the sequence is complete if, and only if, $1 \le t < 2$. If $\alpha < 1$, the sequence is not complete for any $t > 0$. As for those partial results for the region where it's not fully known yet, one can observe that for any compact set contained in that region, determining whether the sequence is complete for all pairs $(t, \alpha)$ in that set, in principle reduces to a finite search. I have therefore done some coding and can show the following computer-assisted result: If $\alpha \le 1.4$, the sequence is complete for all $t \le 3$. If $\alpha \le 1.3$, the sequence is complete for all $t \le 5$. If $\alpha \le 1.2$, the sequence is complete for all $t \le 10$. If $\alpha \le 1.1$, the sequence is complete for all $t \le 50$. These ranges are mostly a proof of concept, and for anyone interested it shouldn't be too hard to extend them further. The final result I prove in my paper is that the sequence is complete whenever $1 < \alpha \le 1 + \frac{1}{\lceil t \rceil + 2 \lceil \sqrt{t} \rceil}$, which (when we plot the values of $t$ and $\alpha$) is the first result that implies the completeness of the sequence for a region of infinite area!

How is this going as of Dec 2025? I skimmed through Graham's and your paper. It seems like all cases (both complete and entirely complete) are settled except for this $1 < \alpha < \varphi$ and $t > \min(2/\alpha, 3/\alpha^2)$ region, where "entirely complete" is settled (in the negative), but "complete" is open. I also understand that you have an algorithm that can prove completeness in this region by covering it with squares and going one square at a time. (Although when you wrote "For any compact set $C \subset U$ it is in principle possible to tile the entirety of $C$ with these kinds of squares", it seems like this is an empirical observation rather than you knowing for certain that these squares must exist? - Correct me if I'm wrong.) The key tool seems to be Lemma 5. Regardless of whether this is known for certain or just an empirical observation, it seems like you feel that an automated proof to some moderately large $t$ appears possible to generate. Has this been done? If not, I think it would be interesting to do this up to $t \leq \text{some number}$, gather the results we have and the algorithm for future people to run, as a partial resolution of the problem. Because it seems like there is a bit of progress being done here since Graham already, but maybe some further idea is still required to settle $t$ large? (So it would make sense to publish this as a progress checkpoint.)

I have finally uploaded it to the arXiv and updated the link in my comment. I initially planned to combine my paper with some other projects I had been working on (hence the delay in posting and response), but I have mostly abandoned this. So in the end not much has changed over the past $6$ months, but okay. As for your questions, most of my responses can be found in the above comment. The relevant GitHub file including the code I used for the computer-assisted result can be found here. And finally, I certainly can't prove that it is possible to tile the entire (relevant part of the) plane with these squares, as otherwise I would be able to fully resolve this problem ;) But so far I have seen no reason to expect the existence of a mathematical barrier that would stop us extending the proven region further and further. Hope that clears it up!
Oh, um, thanks! Congrats for the uploaded paper.

Concerning the problem of parity of integer parts of power of 3/2, Dubickas and Novikas proved in their 2005 paper, Integer parts of powers of rational numbers (Theorem 1), that infinitely often these must be divisible by at least one of 2,5,7,11.

Suppose we take $b_k$ to be the deduplicated increasing tail of $\lfloor t\alpha^n\rfloor$, and look at its subset sums. If for some $K_0$ there is an interval $[A,A+L]\subseteq\Sigma_{K_0}$ with cardinality $L+1\ge b_{K_0+1}$, then once the eventual overlap $b_{m+1}\le 2b_m-1$ (which eventually holds for large $m$ when $1<\alpha<2$, up to sporadic floor effects, and even $b_{m+2}\le b_{m+1}+b_m-1$ for $1<\alpha<\varphi$) has started to hold, the translate–overlap mechanism forces that from some point onward all sufficiently large integers are covered. So for $\alpha<\varphi$ it seems the whole problem reduces to finding one finite seed interval beyond the eventual regime. What I am not sure about is whether this reduction is completely sound, or whether there could be a gap before the overlap inequality takes effect. Do we know that some seed must appear for every $t>0$ and $1<\alpha<\varphi$, or could it in principle fail entirely in the finite pre-asymptotic window? The seed condition I think has to be expressed in cardinalities ($L+1\ge b_{K_0+1}$), otherwise there is an off-by-one issue. Deduplication is required too, since the problem is about distinct integers. Is there an argument that guarantees a seed in all cases, or is this the open difficulty? If the latter, what kinds of computational (python) directions or heuristics might probe at guaranteeing the seed interval (e.g. generate sequence -> compute sums -> detect first covering interval)? Thank you.

Proving that such a seed always exists is essentially the difficulty, yes. Furthermore, you are correct that -as stated- the problem requires distinct integers of the form $\left \lfloor t \alpha^n \right \rfloor$, but that is not what Erdős and Graham wrote (or what I believe they intended), and also not what is assumed in Graham's 1964 paper. Every element $s_n = \left \lfloor t \alpha^n \right \rfloor$ is allowed to be used once, even if $s_n = s_m$ for some $m \neq n$. For example, Theorem 2 in Graham's paper implies in particular that the sequence with $t = \frac{2}{3}$ and $\alpha = 1.7$ (which has $s_1 = s_2 = 1$ and $s_3 = 3$) is entirely complete, which would not be true if we are not allowed to use both $s_1$ and $s_2$. For what it's worth, I am currently writing up some slight extensions of Graham's results that I will hopefully link to in the coming week.

The script below shows that for $t=1,\alpha=1.5$ and $\alpha=\varphi$ all integers up to $200{,}000$ are representable, while for $t=2,\alpha=1.4$ a few small numbers ($1,4,6,\dots$) are missing even though the density is very high. It is only a computation, not a proof, but it hopefully illustrates the behaviour discussed in Problem 349. import math def seq(t, a, n=40): return [int(t * a**k) for k in range(1, n+1)] def cover(s, L=200000): r = [0] * (L+1) r[0] = 1 for a in s: for x in range(L, a-1, -1): r[x] |= r[x - a] return sum(r[1:]) / L, [i for i in range(1, L+1) if not r[i]] for t, a in [(1, 1.5), (1, (1 + 5**0.5) / 2), (2, 1.4)]: s = seq(t, a) d, m = cover(s) print(f"\nt={t}, α≈{round(a, 3)}") print("First terms:", s[:12]) print(f"Density up to 200k: {d:.6f}") print("First missing:", m[:10] if m else "None") We get: t=1, α≈1.5 First terms: [1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 17, 25, 38, 57, 86, 129] Density up to 200k: 1.000000 First missing: None t=1, α≈1.618 First terms: [1, 2, 4, 6, 11, 17, 29, 46, 76, 122, 199, 321] Density up to 200k: 1.000000 First missing: None t=2, α≈1.4 First terms: [2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 21, 29, 41, 57, 80, 113] Density up to 200k: 0.999975 First missing: [1, 4, 6, 11, 16] It seems from these checks that the behaviour of $\lfloor t \alpha^n \rfloor$ could hinge on how closely it shadows a Fibonacci-type recurrence, with small floor-errors making it look complete below the golden ratio. The code just needs correct indentation, correct formatting available here: https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1nS-K7ednSSD2JoOD-BKwrnoXOTini1xX?usp=sharing
